title: “Supply chains across APAC are increasingly exposed to geopolitical uncertainty, evolving trade policies, regulatory fragmentation”
summary: “Supply chains across APAC are increasingly exposed to geopolitical uncertainty, evolving trade policies, regulatory fragmentation”
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
“Supply chains across APAC are increasingly exposed to geopolitical uncertainty, evolving trade policies, regulatory fragmentation”

July 1, 2026 | Wednesday | Opinion | By Narayan Kulkarni

As the Asia-Pacific region accelerates its rise as a global hub for biopharmaceutical manufacturing and innovation, companies are increasingly investing in local capabilities to support growing demand for biologics, vaccines, and advanced therapies. Against this backdrop, Merck Life Science’s expanded Peenya facility in Bengaluru has emerged as a strategic manufacturing and supply chain hub within the company’s global network. With enhanced capabilities in filtration technologies, bioprocessing solutions, and new product commercialisation, the site is strengthening regional resilience while advancing India’s role in the global life sciences ecosystem. In an exclusive interaction with BioSpectrum Asia, Tony Qiu, Head of APAC Region, Global Plant Operations, ISCO, Merck Life Science, discusses Peenya’s evolution, APAC growth opportunities, supply chain resilience, and the future of biopharma manufacturing in the region.

Since the expansion, the Peenya facility has evolved into a key hub for Merck KGaA—what would you consider its most defining milestones or achievements so far?

The expansion has unlocked substantial new capacity, enabling the successful onboarding of multiple high-value products. Filters for pharma processes have been successfully integrated into the Peenya manufacturing line, reinforcing the site's expertise in filtration-related product manufacturing. Alongside these, MVP Icon Swab (rapid test for hygiene monitoring) production is now underway, demonstrating the site's readiness to support precision-driven manufacturing for food processing environments. Manufacturing these products at Peenya strengthens regional biotech manufacturing capabilities and enhances the reliability of global life science supply chains.

How has the Peenya facility strengthened India’s role within Merck’s global manufacturing and supply chain network?

Our Peenya site in Bengaluru established in 1986, has ever since evolved into a key global manufacturing hub for filtration devices and skids and chromatography systems. Today, the Peenya facility plays a critical role in supporting the APAC region and serves as a benchmark for operational excellence within our Life Science manufacturing network. Beyond producing advanced filtration equipment, the site expanded its capabilities in 2022 to include the manufacturing of prefilter cartridges, strengthening support for pharmaceutical manufacturing processes.

By integrating advanced production capabilities with a strong local ecosystem of skilled vendors, the facility has significantly enhanced manufacturing efficiency and supply chain responsiveness. The Peenya site continues to deliver globally compliant, high-quality life science products that support the evolving needs of the biopharmaceutical industry worldwide.

How does Peenya fit into Merck’s broader APAC manufacturing and growth strategy, particularly as the region scales up in biologics?

Our global R&D teams see Peenya as a key hub for the commercialisation of future product launches in the pipeline. The centre plays a pivotal role in strengthening the company’s capabilities to supply, test, and scale advanced bioprocessing solutions that support the production of biosimilars and vaccines.

By enabling closer alignment with regional manufacturing needs, the facility helps address both local and global demand for high-quality biopharmaceutical components. It is also an important enabler for the scaling of biologics manufacturing across the region, reinforcing our broader APAC growth strategy while supporting faster, more resilient supply for the life sciences industry.

How is Merck positioning APAC differently from other regions in terms of manufacturing priorities and innovation?    

APAC is a fast-growing region where customers increasingly need speed, scalability, and supply reliability. We are positioning the region around an “in-region for region” approach that strengthens local responsiveness while maintaining our global quality standards.

In parallel, APAC is also evolving beyond manufacturing into a more influential innovation ecosystem, driven by rising R&D investment, supportive policy environments, and strong scientific talent pools across markets including China, Singapore, India, South Korea, and Japan. As a result, our priorities in APAC increasingly combine capacity and localization (to improve resilience and lead times) with capability-building and collaboration that help customers scale advanced biopharma modalities more efficiently.  

With countries like China, South Korea, and Singapore investing heavily in biopharma, where does India fit competitively within your APAC strategy?

India is a strategically important market within our broader APAC footprint, one that is evolving beyond the role of a follower market to become a meaningful contributor to growth. The country offers strong advantages in scalability and capability building, complementing more established biopharma hubs such as China, South Korea, and Singapore. While Singapore and South Korea are widely recognised for their strengths in biologics development and advanced manufacturing, India brings distinct value through its deep pool of scientific talent, a well-established pharmaceutical and life sciences ecosystem, and steadily expanding manufacturing and operational infrastructure.

From an APAC business perspective, India is increasingly contributing to operational excellence, cost-effective scaling, and resilient supply chains, particularly at a time when global demand for high-quality life science and bioprocessing solutions continues to rise. As a result, Merck Life Science sees India as a highly promising market that is rapidly maturing in terms of regulatory frameworks, technology adoption, and overall industry capability.

In a competitive landscape, how does Peenya strengthen Merck’s competitive advantage in APAC?

The APAC life sciences and biopharma landscape is becoming increasingly competitive, driven by rapid growth in biologics, the rise of regional CDMOs, and strong investments in local manufacturing across markets such as India, China, and South Korea. At the same time, customers are demanding faster turnaround times, reliable supply, and consistently high-quality, compliant solutions, pushing global players to rethink how they localise and scale their operations.

Against this backdrop, our expansion of the Peenya facility in Bengaluru strengthens its competitive position by enabling closer-to-customer manufacturing and improving responsiveness across key APAC markets. The facility reflects our “in-region for region” strategy, combining India’s cost advantages and deep pool of skilled talent with the company’s global quality and operational standards.

This integration allows us to compete effectively on both scale and innovation while enhancing supply reliability, an increasingly important differentiator in a fragmented and high-growth market. As a result, the Peenya site serves as a strategic lever that balances global expertise with regional agility and resilience.

What are the biggest supply chain vulnerabilities in APAC today, and how is Merck mitigating geopolitical and regulatory risks?

Supply chains across APAC are increasingly exposed to geopolitical uncertainty, evolving trade policies, regulatory fragmentation, and an overreliance on concentrated manufacturing hubs, factors that can disrupt cross-border flows and affect supply reliability. 

In response, we are strengthening traceability and compliance across the supply chain, embedding quality and regulatory readiness at every node, supported by an in-region for-region globally diversified manufacturing network spanning 65 countries and more than 100 manufacturing and distribution sites, alongside investments of over €2 billion since 2020.

At the regional level, initiatives such as the expansion of the Peenya facility in Bengaluru and strategic infrastructure like the Patalganga distribution hub in Maharashtra reinforce our “in-region for region” strategy, reducing dependence on cross-border supply chains. Complementing this approach, the company is diversifying suppliers and leveraging digital, data-driven tools to improve visibility, forecasting, and operational agility, ensuring consistent quality and regulatory compliance while navigating geopolitical and regulatory risks with greater stability.

Narayan Kulkarni
